comment.body:
Let’s take this one step at a time. First question I have is, what is the basis for thinking that these tariffs are simply about forcing a European supported peace deal with Russia/Ukraine? Tariffs have been a major issue for Trump for the last 30-40 years. If you listen to the Tim Dillon interview with Steve Bannon, the motivation seems to be genuine with wanting to make progress with tariffs and to genuinely increase domestic production that supports workers. Here’s the interview:

https://youtu.be/S9aXjWdGWH8?si=Nz_RT7eFPDiIDBWM

Maybe this is just added leverage, and he can make some concessions with the EU if they capitulate on Ukraine, but I highly doubt this would impact Chinese tariffs and I doubt every single one would be dropped.

Second question is, we are going through Q1 earnings season (Jan-March). Just a question for everyone… when were the tariffs enacted? April 2nd, Q2.

So we won’t see any impact on earnings hit for another 3-4 months. How does that play into all this?

Third question, the $9 trillion dollars of debt needs to be refinanced starting in H2 of this year.

Unless the economy roasts through the floor over the next 2 months and Powell drops rates to COVID levels, which it won’t because tariffs haven’t hit the earnings tape, how is that higher interest rate on all that refinanced debt going to effect liquidity and growth prospects moving forward?

Won’t these recession and deflation scares take another 3-6 months to fully play out? I just don’t understand how it’s all going to get wrapped up in 1-2 months.

Moreover, if Trump just cancels all the tariffs on a Russia cease fire deal, then that is going to infuriate the MAGA base and completely destroy his credibly and the ability for the fight to conduct a successful re election campaign.

Just some thoughts here and I’m not saying I’m right.

I’m simply brainstorming, so please don’t have an “autoimmune response” and start attacking me.

I’m just sharing thoughts here.

Let’s take this one step at a time. First question I have is, what is the basis for thinking that these tariffs are simply about forcing a European supported peace deal with Russia/Ukraine? Tariffs have been a major issue for Trump for the last 30-40 years. If you listen to the Tim Dillon interview with Steve Bannon, the motivation seems to be genuine with wanting to make progress with tariffs and to genuinely increase domestic production that supports workers. Here’s the interview:

https://youtu.be/S9aXjWdGWH8?si=Nz_RT7eFPDiIDBWM

Maybe this is just added leverage, and he can make some concessions with the EU if they capitulate on Ukraine, but I highly doubt this would impact Chinese tariffs and I doubt every single one would be dropped.

Second question is, we are going through Q1 earnings season (Jan-March). Just a question for everyone… when were the tariffs enacted? April 2nd, Q2.

So we won’t see any impact on earnings hit for another 3-4 months. How does that play into all this?

Third question, the $9 trillion dollars of debt needs to be refinanced starting in H2 of this year.

Unless the economy roasts through the floor over the next 2 months and Powell drops rates to COVID levels, which it won’t because tariffs haven’t hit the earnings tape, how is that higher interest rate on all that refinanced debt going to effect liquidity and growth prospects moving forward?

Won’t these recession and deflation scares take another 3-6 months to fully play out? I just don’t understand how it’s all going to get wrapped up in 1-2 months.

Moreover, if Trump just cancels all the tariffs on a Russia cease fire deal, then that is going to infuriate the MAGA base and completely destroy his credibly and the ability for the fight to conduct a successful re election campaign.

Just some thoughts here and I’m not saying I’m right.

I’m simply brainstorming, so please don’t have an “autoimmune response” and start attacking me.

1.) Its not entirely about the peace deal, just a pressure tactic. The tariffs are there to bring manufacturing back to some extent. I believe that is the ultimate goal. EU knows that if ceasfire talks go in Russia's favor then Russia will start to move onto the balkans (his grand dream to rebuild the USSR). With trump tarriffs on them there is added pressure to sort of "bend the knee"

2.) No effects if there is a practically a trade embargo, thats why im surprised there's been no coverage at the ports. Market generally believes this is a temporary bump in trumps grand plan since tarriffs have been known to not benefit anyone (any history book would tell you that) so for an entire administration to go about as that as their econoic policy is wild. If he lifts it then any bad earnings get written off as a "itll be better next time"

3.) Tarriffs will affect inflation and slow consumer spending. Its not about earnings, its about the consumer. If the debt can't be refinanced you end up in a debt spiral. I think this happened to japan post WW2?

comment.body:
I don't think the tariffs scare will be about earnings (that's for the future), simply jobs will be affected sooner. Starting from ports, logistics, etc. Less products, people will jump in like on eggs or sugar in Poland few years ago. That's when the public will get angry. And I saw somewhere it takes around 30 days for a ship to travel to NY, and a little bit more to LA. Or other way around, I don't remember now. 

So after the month (02.05?) there will be less ships. Until they make this tariffs deal and they start coming again in regular numbers. 
This will trigger the panic for a dip which institutions will buy, if the puzzles make a happy picture in the end. 

But how far we will go up before the panic? Alma is constantly thinking. That is why she gave her weekly and "final" target numbers few times this week. Updating isn't bad. It should help you. 
Things are evolving. More conclusions are being made. The picture is clearing out. 

And what is very important here, is that not a single person on this chat can do the same this quickly. Unless that guy she's talking with is lurking as one of the students. Maybe even helping from time to time cause he's a bored dinosaur and a great friend (hello there, any intern positions open?) 

 I think I'm not saying this about only myself. We don't have info, we don't have knowledge, we don't have exp and we don't have the skill. Yet, we do it slowly, and sometimes successfully. I think that's why some people get confused. That's why I was confused. Required tempo is different here than anywhere else. 
... But... reducing number of trades per week from x to 10, helped me get 50% more depo since Feb when I joined, but also gave me more time to think. Read this shit through. Over and over again. Too many times I took wrong trades cause I misinterpreted the post at start. One word wrong and bam. But I got explanations every time. That last part started to make me feel motivated. We all should feel motivated to learn. And who knows best, how and what should we learn to be successful traders? 

Alma already started positioning into opex  and eom puts. Already hedged those. We get the updates, we take it day by day.

The detail: Service Fee on Chinese Vessel Operators and Vessel Owners of China:
Effective as of April 17, 2025, a fee in the amount of $0 per net ton for the arriving vessel.
Effective as of October 14, 2025, a fee in the amount of $50 per net ton for the arriving vessel.
Effective as of April 17, 2026, a fee in the amount of $80 per net ton for the arriving vessel.
Effective as of April 17, 2027, a fee in the amount of $110 per net ton for the arriving vessel.
Effective as of April 17, 2028, a fee in the amount of $140 per net ton for the arriving vessel.
The fee will be charged up to five times per year, per vessel. The register did not break out the price per container.
Service fees on vessel operators of Chinese-Built vessels is lower.
Effective as of: April 17, 2025, a fee in the amount of $0 for each container discharged.
Effective as of October 14, 2025, a fee in the amount of $18 per net ton ($120 per container)
Effective as of April 17, 2026, a fee in the amount of $23 per net ton ($153 per container)
Effective as of April 17, 2027, a fee in the amount of $28 per net ton ($195 per container)

comment.body:
Charlie McElligott, a market strategist, presents a case for a tactical pullback in gold prices due to crowded positioning, historical signals, and evolving market dynamics. While maintaining a long-term positive outlook on gold as a portfolio diversifier, he highlights several indicators pointing to a potential short-term correction.
Gold has benefited significantly from themes favoring alternatives to the U.S. dollar and a weakening dollar narrative, resulting in heavily crowded long positions. This rally stems from multiple factors: gold serving as a hedge against a declining dollar, real yields, risk assets, and as a safe haven amid policy uncertainty and geopolitical tensions. However, the persistent dollar weakness is tied to private investors’ overweight positions in U.S. assets and currency, which are now under pressure.
McElligott identifies three key signals supporting a tactical pullback. First, a composite regional Fed planned capital expenditure index recently dropped below -4, a threshold historically linked to recession signals in five out of six instances since 1975. Following such events, economically sensitive assets like small-cap stocks tend to perform poorly, and gold typically underperforms over the next two months. This signal often aligns with a temporary dollar rally and rising Treasury yields before yields decline.
Second, extreme flows in the GLD ETF indicate potential overshoot. A two-week inflow at the 95th percentile was followed by a one-week outflow at the 5th percentile, suggesting a "mania" phase and monetization risk. Historically, such flow reversals have preceded significant gold pullbacks within two months.
Third, gold’s price momentum shows signs of exhaustion, with prices trading approximately 25% above the 200-day moving average—a rare outlier. Past instances of such momentum extremes have consistently led to poor gold returns over the subsequent two months.
The broader macroeconomic environment adds context, with trade policy uncertainty and tariffs already dampening business investment visibility. Political signals of disruption have fueled perceptions of declining confidence in dollar-denominated assets, boosting gold’s appeal. However, recent market capitulations have temporarily alleviated these concerns.
Despite these short-term risks, McElligott remains optimistic about gold’s long-term role as a hedge against risk assets and expected shifts in monetary and fiscal policy. He anticipates strong buying interest if a meaningful pullback occurs.
In summary, technical, positioning, and macroeconomic factors suggest a window for a near-term gold correction. While the structural case for gold remains robust, investors should brace for potential short-term downside based on historical patterns and current market signals.

I don’t think that putin will let them do this ). One crucial point is that Ukraine  will not give up on recognising Crimea as it is written in their military doctrine. If investors are currently skeptical about the US and shifting focus to BRICS, and tariffs are going to bring both inflation and economic slowdown, Powell can't risk an early QE without fueling even more inflation and pushing investors even further away.

comment.body:
Waller and the FED are looking for… drum roll… massive drop in the labor market in order to step in. On a per hourly basis, it has already been getting hammered.

https://youtu.be/vGnWP_x7Rzs?si=UnGt37AJn-Kre7TA

Joseph Wang in this video wrote a book called “Central Banking 101”. I got the audio book… bad mistake 😂. I’m gonna grab a physical copy as well and jump back into it.

He ironically covers many of Alma’s points, and mentions more, including:

1. Fed needing weak employment numbers to fully bail out.
2. The lows are not likely in
3. Container shipments (research done by Craig Fuller who studies logistics) are declining significantly.
4. There will be a delayed supply shock on store shelves signifying an economic impact beginning in June or July.
5. On tariffs for countries excluding China, companies are splitting the levies into thirds between the company, the importer, and the consumer.
6. Large companies have some margin to burn on absorbing levies. That means, LOWER JUSTIFIABLE PE RATIOS MOVING FORWARD.
7. Bigger companies (presumably retailers like Amazon/Walmart) are going to sacrifice profits short term to capitalize on gaining market share against companies who have to pass levies on to consumers. This should lead to very buyable dips.
8. Tariffs will cause a 1% increase in home prices. Compound that with a weakening economy and the FED’s need for lower employment numbers, I might need to re-short KBH 😂 
9. Less travel and discretionary spending. Probably not good for restaurants either.

I don't think Ukraine and Russia will reach a ceasefire agreement. Of course, in reality, they don't necessarily fight battles every single day — it’s more like occasionally throwing a bomb here and there. Either way, the war of attrition will eventually wear Ukraine down.
Europe, on the surface, has to show support for Ukraine, especially Germany, which is happily rearming itself. Germany’s military-industrial strength is very powerful, but historically, their arms orders were suppressed by the United States, because within NATO, the U.S. paid the bill and thus had the leverage to prioritize selling their own weapons to the world.
Moreover, Putin has absolutely no respect for Trump or people like Witkoff. Americans say Witkoff was just someone who used to collect rent with a gun. Putin sees himself as an emperor, while Trump is merely a businessman — they are not in the same class. Putin certainly doesn’t need to outright reject America; he can just find some excuse to make negotiations fall through.
In the meantime, Putin will slowly nibble away at Ukraine. Ukraine is already in ruins, and as time passes, when people realize there’s no hope of rebuilding their homes, they’ll move away. In a deserted, uninhabited land, who would stay to defend it?

comment.body:
Here's a structured analysis based on the information provided and current economic trends:

---

### 1. **China Shipment Disruptions**
- **Immediate Collapse**: A near-stop in shipments within two weeks aligns with escalations in trade tensions (e.g., new tariffs, export controls). Recent U.S. tariffs on Chinese EVs, steel, and semiconductors (May 2024) could explain abrupt supply-chain pullbacks.
- **Supply-Chain Chaos**: Ships returning mid-voyage suggest importers are rushing to avoid tariffs or preempt embargoes. This risks port congestion, contract disputes, and inventory shortfalls.

---

### 2. **Consumer Impact**
- **Short-Term Price Spikes**: Electronics, auto parts, and holiday goods (e.g., back-to-school inventory) may see immediate shortages, driving up prices.
- **Long-Term Inflation**: If tariffs persist, retailers will pass costs to consumers. The U.S. imports ~18% of goods from China; sectors like apparel and machinery are especially vulnerable.

---

### 3. **Stock Market Reactions**
- **Transportation Sector**: Companies like ZIM, Matson, and port operators (e.g., SSA Marine) could plummet on volume declines. Shipping rates (Freightos Index) may drop short-term but rebound if capacity is cut.
- **Retail and Manufacturing**: Target, Dollar General, and auto manufacturers relying on Chinese components (e.g., Tesla) may warn of margin pressures, triggering sell-offs.
- **Winners**: Domestic manufacturers (e.g., US Steel) and alternative sourcing hubs (Vietnam, India) could rally on substitution demand.

---

### 4. **News Cycle Timeline**
- **Week 1**: Trade journalists and logistics firms (e.g., Flexport, FreightWaves) will report unusual ship movements. 
- **Week 2**: Mainstream outlets (WSJ, Bloomberg) will link the halt to policy leaks or negotiations. Expect mentions in Fed speeches or White House briefings.
- **Week 3-4**: Earnings pre-announcements from affected companies; possible White House tariff exemptions to calm markets.

---

### 5. **Key Triggers to Monitor**
- **China’s Retaliation**: Export bans on rare earths (used in EVs/tech) or agricultural purchases (soybeans) would escalate tensions.
- **CPI Data**: June/July inflation prints may spike, forcing the Fed to delay rate cuts.
- **Corporate Guidance**: Walmart, Home Depot, and Best Buy may issue warnings in late July earnings calls.

---

**Actionable Takeaway**: Prepare for volatility in consumer discretionary stocks and container-shipping ETFs (e.g., SEA). Hedge with inflation-protected securities (TIPS) and domestic industrial equities. Monitor real-time AIS shipping data (via MarineTraffic) for confirmation of the trend.

"Retail and consumer‐discretionary stocks will show warning signals: companies posting inventory write‐downs or guidance cuts should see immediate share‐price weakness. Short‐dated corporate credit could widen as cash flows are squeezed; bond‐market volatility may rise as funding costs climb amidst margin stress.
The timing aligns with the May 2 de minimis tariff change—another potential catalyst—so investors should brace for renewed volatility around both retail‐sales data and customs‐fee implementations.
This directly leads to a supply-constrained environment, causing a new inflationary cycle.
Why? Because due to disrupted supply chains and reduced imports we get supply shortages. Tariffs and increased input costs cause rising production costs. And inventory depletion lead to empty shelves and potential price hikes. So basically, current order freeze and booking cancellations will translate into empty shelves. Coupled with a freight-volumes collapse and ensuing layoffs, this sets up a full-scale supply-shock recession that will reverberate through GDP, employment, and cost-push inflation (higher consumer prices, margin compression) at the same time". https://open.substack.com/pub/stochvoltrader/p/market-analysis?r=4jmkho&utm_campaign=post&utm_medium=web&showWelcomeOnShare=false

comment.body:
Here's a structured analysis based on the information provided and current economic trends:

---

### 1. **China Shipment Disruptions**
- **Immediate Collapse**: A near-stop in shipments within two weeks aligns with escalations in trade tensions (e.g., new tariffs, export controls). Recent U.S. tariffs on Chinese EVs, steel, and semiconductors (May 2024) could explain abrupt supply-chain pullbacks.
- **Supply-Chain Chaos**: Ships returning mid-voyage suggest importers are rushing to avoid tariffs or preempt embargoes. This risks port congestion, contract disputes, and inventory shortfalls.

---

### 2. **Consumer Impact**
- **Short-Term Price Spikes**: Electronics, auto parts, and holiday goods (e.g., back-to-school inventory) may see immediate shortages, driving up prices.
- **Long-Term Inflation**: If tariffs persist, retailers will pass costs to consumers. The U.S. imports ~18% of goods from China; sectors like apparel and machinery are especially vulnerable.

---

### 3. **Stock Market Reactions**
- **Transportation Sector**: Companies like ZIM, Matson, and port operators (e.g., SSA Marine) could plummet on volume declines. Shipping rates (Freightos Index) may drop short-term but rebound if capacity is cut.
- **Retail and Manufacturing**: Target, Dollar General, and auto manufacturers relying on Chinese components (e.g., Tesla) may warn of margin pressures, triggering sell-offs.
- **Winners**: Domestic manufacturers (e.g., US Steel) and alternative sourcing hubs (Vietnam, India) could rally on substitution demand.

---

### 4. **News Cycle Timeline**
- **Week 1**: Trade journalists and logistics firms (e.g., Flexport, FreightWaves) will report unusual ship movements. 
- **Week 2**: Mainstream outlets (WSJ, Bloomberg) will link the halt to policy leaks or negotiations. Expect mentions in Fed speeches or White House briefings.
- **Week 3-4**: Earnings pre-announcements from affected companies; possible White House tariff exemptions to calm markets.

---

### 5. **Key Triggers to Monitor**
- **China’s Retaliation**: Export bans on rare earths (used in EVs/tech) or agricultural purchases (soybeans) would escalate tensions.
- **CPI Data**: June/July inflation prints may spike, forcing the Fed to delay rate cuts.
- **Corporate Guidance**: Walmart, Home Depot, and Best Buy may issue warnings in late July earnings calls.

---

**Actionable Takeaway**: Prepare for volatility in consumer discretionary stocks and container-shipping ETFs (e.g., SEA). Hedge with inflation-protected securities (TIPS) and domestic industrial equities. Monitor real-time AIS shipping data (via MarineTraffic) for confirmation of the trend.

comment.body:
In China, the concept of "mianzi" (面子) revolves heavily around maintaining dignity, status, and respect in the eyes of others.
If someone is publicly humiliated in China, it’s considered "losing face" (丢脸) — and that's extremely serious. It can damage not only a person's personal pride but also ruin their chances in social or business relationships. In China, relationships (guanxi, 关系) are incredibly important: your network often determines your success. Losing face can therefore be a kind of social death.
